note: Mathlib/Logic/Relation.lean:896-898 theorem church_rosser: 'A sufficient condition for the Church-Rosser property' -- an abstract-rewriting-system (any relation r satisfying local confluence) generalization of the classical theorem, which is specifically about lambda-calculus beta-reduction; Mathlib has no lambda calculus, so this is the generic confluence machinery the concept specializes.
verify_note: Relation.church_rosser confirmed at Mathlib/Logic/Relation.lean:896-898 (docstring 'A sufficient condition for the Church-Rosser property'); it's generic abstract-rewriting confluence machinery (local-confluence hypothesis -> Join of ReflTransGen), while the Wikidata concept (confirmed via Special:EntityData) is specifically the lambda-calculus beta-reduction theorem; grep confirms Mathlib has no lambda-calculus formalization, so 'generalization' (not 'exact') honestly describes the relation.

note: Mathlib/Analysis/SpecialFunctions/Trigonometric/Cotangent.lean:236-240 theorem cot_series_rep, in `section MittagLeffler`, docstring explicitly calls the cotangent partial-fraction expansion 'also known as the Mittag-Leffler expansion' -- this proves ONE specific instance (cot's poles) rather than the general Mittag-Leffler theorem (existence of a meromorphic fn with arbitrary prescribed principal parts), so related not exact/special_case.
verify_note: cot_series_rep confirmed at Cotangent.lean:231-237 inside `section MittagLeffler`; the module docstring (lines 18-25, not the cited 236-240 -- the row's line range is a few lines off, pointing at the proof tail/section-end instead) does call this expansion 'also known as the Mittag-Leffler expansion', and the theorem only proves the cotangent-specific partial-fraction series, not the general existence theorem for prescribed poles (confirmed via Wikidata description) -- 'related' rather than 'exact' is the honest, non-overclaiming call despite the imprecise line citation.

note: Mathlib/Combinatorics/SimpleGraph/Walk/Chord.lean:44-46 def IsChordless: 'a walk with no chords' -- the primitive chord/chordless-walk vocabulary a chordal-graph definition would be built from (chordal = every cycle of length >=4 has a chord), but the graph-level property (no long induced/chordless cycle) itself is not defined anywhere in Mathlib -- grep for 'Chordal' across Mathlib returns nothing.
verify_note: SimpleGraph.Walk.IsChordless confirmed at Walk/Chord.lean:44-46 ('a walk with no chords'); grep of all Mathlib for 'chordal' (case-insensitive) returns zero hits, confirming the graph-level chordal property is genuinely absent; IsChordless is only the walk-level chord vocabulary a chordal-graph definition would be built from, so 'related' at low confidence (not 'exact' or 'special_case') is an honest, conservative characterization.
